text: API design is the process of defining how software components communicate. A well-designed API is intuitive, consistent, predictable, and hard to misuse. The goal is that a developer who has never seen the API can guess how it works based on patterns established by the first few endpoints or functions they encounter.

REST API design principle — use nouns not verbs in endpoint paths. The HTTP method defines the action, not the URL. Correct: GET /users, POST /users, DELETE /users/123. Incorrect: GET /getUsers, POST /createUser. The resource is the noun, the method is the verb.

REST API design principle — use proper HTTP status codes consistently. 200 OK for successful retrieval. 201 Created for successful resource creation. 400 Bad Request for invalid input. 401 Unauthorized for missing authentication. 403 Forbidden for insufficient permissions. 404 Not Found for missing resources. 409 Conflict for state conflicts. 500 Internal Server Error for unexpected failures. Consistent status codes let clients handle errors programmatically.

API versioning should be designed from day one, not added later. Common strategies: URL versioning (/v1/users) is explicit and easy to route. Header versioning (Accept: application/vnd.api.v1+json) keeps URLs clean but is harder to test in a browser. URL versioning is most practical for public APIs. Never break existing clients — deprecate old versions with notice periods.

API design principle — be consistent across all endpoints. Same naming conventions, same error response format, same pagination pattern, same authentication mechanism. Inconsistency forces developers to memorize special cases and increases integration errors.

Error responses in APIs should be informative and consistent. Return a structured error object, not just a status code. Include: error code (machine readable), message (human readable), and optionally details or field-level validation errors. Example: {error: 'VALIDATION_FAILED', message: 'Invalid input', fields: [{field: 'email', issue: 'invalid format'}]}.

API design in Go — define interfaces at the consumer side, not the producer side. Small focused interfaces are easier to implement and test. Accept interfaces, return structs is a common Go idiom — it gives callers flexibility while keeping return types concrete and inspectable.

Idempotency in API design means calling the same endpoint multiple times produces the same result. GET, PUT, DELETE should be idempotent. POST is typically not. Designing idempotent APIs prevents duplicate operations when clients retry on network failures — critical for payment, order, and state-change endpoints.

API pagination prevents returning unbounded result sets that exhaust memory and slow responses. Common patterns: offset-based pagination (page=2&limit=20) is simple but has issues with data changing between pages. Cursor-based pagination uses an opaque cursor pointing to the last item — more reliable for real-time data. Always set and document default and maximum page sizes.
